Funeral Service In Honor Of Late

Forever

In Our Hearts
Deaconess

Love James (Nwa-Ada)

1954 – 2026
72Years

Thursday, 30 July 2026 · 10am
At Chioma’s Compound, Umugwocha, Ozuzu,
Etche LGA, Rivers State
Thanksgiving Service: Saturday, 1 August 2026
SDA Church, Rumuobiokani, Port Harcourt

Share a Tribute Order of Service
“To live is for Christ, and to die is to gain.” Philippians 1:21
Thursday, 30 July 2026

Order of Service

Officiating Ministers

  • Pastor Solomon Okonu President, Port Harcourt West Conference
  • Pastor Alvin Ikeocha Executive Secretary, Port Harcourt West Conference
  • Pastor Tombari Kpenu Treasurer, Port Harcourt West Conference
  • Pastor Ekezie Chendu Director, Prayer Ministry — Port Harcourt East Conference
  • Pastor Samuel Iroulo President, Port Harcourt East Conference
  • Pastor Nation A. Nation President, Cross River Conference
  • Pastor Richard Woke Ministerial Secretary, Port Harcourt West Conference
  • Pastor Ernest Nwoko Director, Global Mission & Riverine
  • Elder Moses Limejuice Deputy Leader, Degema District
  • Elder Otuomasirichi Ebuzie First Elder, Rumuobiokani Church

Music Ministry

Song Leaders: Akachukwu Eke, Godson Chibueze, Choir Leaders/Designates

Organists: Engr. Precious Amaugo, Lucky Walson

Choirs: Oro-Evo District Choir, Degema District Choir, Light House Choir

Soloists: Akachukwu Eke, Nathan David Adiele

Schedule of Events

  • Funeral Service: Thursday, 30 July 2026 · Chioma’s Compound, Ozuzu
  • Interment: Immediately after the Funeral Service · Chioma’s Compound
  • Reception: Immediately after the Interment · Chioma’s Compound
  • Thanksgiving Service: Saturday, 1 August 2026 · SDA Church, Rumuobiokani, Port Harcourt

Part I — The Funeral Service

  1. Arrival: Brethren, friends and sympathizers
  2. Song Service: Song Leaders & Sound
  3. Special Song: Nathan David Adiele — Pie Jesu
  4. Processional: Officiating ministers and the bereaved family
  5. Introduction of Officiating Ministers
  6. Opening Hymn: SDAH 442 — “How Sweet Are the Tidings”
  7. Scripture Reading
  8. Opening Prayer
  9. Lying-in-State: Family / Villagers / Friends / Church / Ministers
  10. Special Song: Nathan David Adiele — “Time to Say Goodbye”
  11. Biography: Engr. Imoh James (for the family)
  12. Special Song: Degema District Choir
  13. Special Song: Oro-Evo District Choir
  14. General Song: Selected Choristers with Light House Choir — He Will Hold Me Fast
  15. Sermon: Pastor Solomon Okonu
  16. Prayer for the Family
  17. Special Song: Light House Choir — When Peace Like a River
  18. Closing Hymn: SDAH 50 — “Abide with Me”
  19. Benediction
  20. Special Song: AWM, Oro-Evo District
  21. Recessional: Casket wheeled out, followed by the immediate family and officiating ministers

Part II — The Graveside Service (Interment)

  1. Opening Hymn: SDAH 205 — “Gleams of the Golden Morning”
  2. Scripture Reading
  3. Committal Words
  4. Lowering of the Casket
  5. Graveside Prayer
  6. Vote of Thanks
  7. Benediction

Part III — Reception

Served before and during the Funeral Service, and immediately after the interment, at Chioma’s Compound.

Sung In Her Honour

Hymns

Opening Hymn: SDAH 442 — How Sweet Are the Tidings +

How sweet are the tidings that greet the pilgrim’s ear,
As he wanders in exile from home!
Soon, soon will the Savior in glory appear,
And soon will the kingdom come.

Refrain:
He’s coming, coming, coming soon I know,
Coming back to this earth again;
And the weary pilgrims will to glory go,
When the Savior comes to reign.

The mossy old graves where the pilgrims sleep
Shall be open as wide as before,
And the millions that sleep in the mighty deep
Shall live on this earth once more.

There we’ll meet ne’er to part in our happy
Eden home, Sweet songs of redemption we’ll sing;
From the north, from the south, all the ransomed shall come,
And worship our heavenly King.

Hallelujah, Amen! Hallelujah again!
Soon, if faithful, we all shall be there;
O, be watchful, be hopeful, be joyful till then,
And a crown of bright glory we’ll wear.

Closing Hymn: SDAH 50 — Abide With Me +

Abide with me; fast falls the eventide;
The darkness deepens; Lord with me abide!
When other helpers fail and comforts flee,
Help of the helpless, O abide with me.

Swift to its close ebbs out life’s little day;
Earth’s joys grow dim; its glories pass away;
Change and decay in all around I see;
O Thou who changest not, abide with me.

I need Thy presence every passing hour.
What but Thy grace can foil the tempter’s power?
Who, like Thyself, my guide and stay can be?
Through cloud and sunshine, O abide with me.

I fear no foe, with Thee at hand to bless;
Ills have no weight, and tears no bitterness.
Where is death’s sting? Where, grave, thy victory?
I triumph still, if Thou abide with me!

Graveside Hymn: SDAH 205 — Gleams of the Golden Morning +

The golden morning is fast approaching;
Jesus soon will come
To take His faithful and happy children
To their promised home.

Refrain:
O, we see the gleams of the golden morning
piercing thro’ this night of gloom!
O, see the gleams of the golden morning
that will burst the tomb.

The gospel summons will soon be carried
To the nations round;
The Bridegroom then will cease to tarry
And the trumpet sound.

Attended by all the shining angels,
Down the flaming sky
The Judge will come, and will take His people
Where they will not die.

There those loved ones who have long been parted,
Will all meet that day;
The tears of those who are broken hearted
will be wiped away.

General Hymn — He Will Hold Me Fast +

When I fear my faith will fail,
Christ will hold me fast;
When the tempter would prevail,
He can hold me fast!

Refrain:
He will hold me fast, He will hold me fast;
For my Savior loves me so,
He will hold me fast.

I could never keep my hold,
He must hold me fast;
For my love is often cold,
He must hold me fast.

I am precious in His sight,
He will hold me fast;
Those He saves are His delight,
He will hold me fast.

He’ll not let my soul be lost,
Christ will hold me fast;
Bought by Him at such a cost,
He will hold me fast.

Born 1954 · Died 2026

Her Life Story

Deaconess Love James · 1954–2026

“She lived with grace, gave with love, and rested in faith.”

There are lives that leave a mark so deep that even silence speaks of them. Deaconess Love James was such a life — a woman of extraordinary warmth, quiet strength, and unshakeable faith. To know her was to encounter love in its most selfless form, felt in every meal cooked with care, every hymn sung at dawn, and every door held open to a soul in need. She was mother, farmer, deaconess, provider, and friend — the first daughter of her father’s house, carrying that position not as a title but as a calling. She leaves behind five children, grandchildren, siblings, and a family stretched across Ozuzu and Ukanafun, all richer for having known her. This biography is a small monument to a large life.

Birth and Early Days

Deaconess Love James was born in 1954 in Umugwuocha, Ozuzu, Etche Local Government Area, Rivers State, Nigeria. The first child of Late Mr. Morrisson Chimezie Chioma and Late Mrs. Beatrice Oghuanya Chioma (née Nwecheform), a position that earned her the cherished name Nwa-Ada. She was the eldest of five siblings, two sisters and three brothers, all raised together in the Chioma compound. As first daughter, she learned early to give before she was taught to ask, and to nurture before she fully understood the word. A preteen when the civil war struck, she was forced to grow beyond her years, a trial that forged the resilience she carried all her life. The close-knit community of Umugwuocha shaped her into the woman she became — rooted, generous, and deeply tied to her people.

Education

She received her early education in Ozuzu, attending primary school within her home community. Though formal schooling had its seasons and limits, her truest education came from life itself — the discipline of a large family home, the rhythms of communal living, and the industry demanded of a first daughter. Whatever the classroom could not give, she fashioned for herself. Her greatest academic investment was made through her children; she ensured that each of her five received a solid education, often at a sacrifice she never spoke of.

Family

Her journey into family life began at a young age and was marked by challenges that helped shape her resilience and strength of character. Through those early experiences, she developed a quiet determination and deep sense of responsibility that would define the rest of her life. From the very beginning, she embraced motherhood with courage, selfless devotion, and unwavering love, laying the foundation for the remarkable woman she would become.

Her marriage to Late Mr. Mfon James brought solidity and purpose to her world. Together they built a home, full of mouths fed, songs sung, and children accounted for. To that union were born six children, two of whom are now late. The four who remain today — three sons and a daughter — carry her iron and her warmth in equal measure. In her grandchildren, her strong gene lives on, each one cherished with the same fondness she poured into her own.

She was beloved within the wider Chioma family and maintained a warm presence among her husband’s people in Ukanafun, bridging two families with characteristic grace. When Late Mr. Mfon James passed on 11 September 2001, she was torn, she was broken, but she did not crumble. Her children were her light. She drew herself upright, pulled them close, and continued to build. The loss was great; her love was greater.

Occupation

A farmer by trade and temperament, Deaconess Love James understood that patient hands yield lasting harvests. She worked her land with dedication, and it was through this quiet, consistent labour that many of her children’s needs were met. She was also a trader — industrious without being restless, purposeful without being proud — and she learned tailoring, always willing to acquire skills that lightened the family burden. People returned to her not only for what she sold, but for who she was: honest, fair, and warm. Her home was always open, and her table was never bare. Those who came close found in her a place of solace.

Among her most enduring legacies was her cooking. Her Egusi soup, okazi, and bitter leaf soups were matters of legend — rich, layered, and entirely her own. Her Afang, carefully modernized over the years, was a signature. Her special porridge and the daily variety she set before her children spoke of a mother who knew that feeding a child well is one of the most eloquent forms of love. Even when resources were stretched, she found a way.

Christian Life

Faith was not something Deaconess Love James wore on Saturdays — it was woven into the fabric of every day. A devoted member of the Seventh-day Adventist Church at Rumuobiokani, she served faithfully as a Deaconess. She sang in the church choir in Ozuzu, and her love of music was prayer in another form. Every morning, she would lead her household in the hymn “Lord, in the Morning,” her voice steady and sure, setting the tone for the day with gratitude before the world could crowd it out, and every evening, as the light softened, she would sing “Abide with Me” — a ritual that her children now carry in their memories like a compass. She held in her heart an archive of hymns and gospel folk songs that seemed without end, singing as she rested, as she worked, and as she held her children close. Those who knew her can still hear her. That is the gift of a singing soul — it does leave a lasting memory.

Social Life

Deaconess Love James did not live for herself — her world expanded naturally to embrace all who drew near. She was a keeper of people, the neighbour others turned to when things fell apart, the friend sought out simply to feel less alone. She understood that community is built not in grand gestures but in the accumulation of small, consistent kindnesses, and she gave those freely. Music was a constant companion. Her warmth drew people, her generosity of spirit kept them. She was, in every sense of the word, beloved.

Her Final Days

In the years following her husband’s passing, Deaconess Love James bore with quiet courage a declining body — arthritis, high blood pressure, and in time, a diagnosis of Alzheimer’s disease, a condition that slowly dimmed the edges of memory but never extinguished the warmth at her core. Through it all, her children and family surrounded her with unwavering care, so she never faced the darkness alone.

On Sunday, 18 May 2026, she fell ill and was taken to hospital, then brought back home. Her condition worsened through the week. On the mornings of Saturday 23 May and Sunday 24 May, she was admitted to Good Heart Medical Consultant Hospital, where every effort was made to stabilize her. When her condition exceeded what the facility could manage, she was referred to Braithwaite Memorial Hospital (BMH), where she was admitted for further care. In the late hours of Monday, 25 May 2026, before midnight, Deaconess Love James breathed her last, gently, as she had lived, carried by a faith that had never once failed her.

She was loved in life. She was tended in sickness. She is celebrated in death.

“I have fought a good fight, I have finished my course, I have kept the faith.” — 2 Timothy 4:7
“Chukwu gozie gi, duzie gi, chebe gi. Mee ka ihu Ya nwuakwasi gi” — Rest in perfect peace.
“May God bless you, guide you, keep you. The Lord makes His face shine on you.” — Numbers 6:24–25
Words From Those Who Loved Her

Tributes

To a woman of impact

Mr. Israel Chioma

Tribute to My Lovely Mother

To my lovely and generous sweet mother, Dada, as we all fondly called you.

Death, oh death, you are wicked. Although you are a reality of life, your coming was untimely.

The last time I met you, my lovely mother, you said nothing to me. Instead, you only shed tears. Deep within my heart, I felt that something was wrong.

My only consolation is that you are resting in the Lord. Heaven has gained a beautiful soul, but we will miss you dearly.

May your lovely and gentle soul continue to rest in the Lord until we meet to part no more. Rest in perfect peace, dear Mother. You will forever remain in our heart.

— Your son, Israel Chioma

Eld. Engr. Godwin James

Tribute to Our Beloved Mother

With gratitude to God, we celebrate the life of our beloved mother, a woman of unwavering faith, boundless love, and remarkable kindness. She was a devoted Christian whose life reflected the values of compassion, humility, and service to God and humanity.

As a mother, she was caring, selfless, and deeply committed to the well-being of her family. Her love, guidance, prayers, and sacrifices shaped our lives and will remain a lasting source of inspiration.

Though her passing leaves a great void in our hearts, we find comfort in the legacy of faith and love she leaves behind. We thank God for the privilege of having her as our mother and cherish the precious memories we shared.

Mum, your love will forever live in our hearts, and your example will continue to guide us. Rest peacefully in the Lord until we meet again.

— Your Loving Son, Elder Godwin James
“To live in hearts we leave behind is not to die.”Thomas Campbell

Shepherdess Engr. Patience Nwoko

My Mother, My Love, My Foundation

My mother. My love. My foundation. My strongest earthly pillar. My push to thrive. My forever crush. My biggest support system. My hospitality champion. My hygiene prof. My biggest fan in life. My brave fighter. My beautiful damsel. My queen. My full paycheck. My inspiration. My reflection. My partner in disagreement. My dynamic chef. The one who makes my world beautiful.

Her smile drives away pain and hates. Her prayers make me forget every painful past. Her dreams keep me pushing… Her simplicity, my charm. Her prayers, my strength. Her dreams are my legacy. My world… O life!

Her children were “her everything”. She was satisfied with life and her children, a home builder, an expert in providing basic life necessities, a carrier of responsibilities, my father’s strongest assets, my brothers’ peace, my husband’s bond, a deep lover, never covetous to anyone, a home maker. A team player, an affectionate listener.

Growing up, her love for me felt so obsessive, but selfless, couldn’t comprehend how she could love me that much and yet allowed me to find my own path, sow, grow and glow. My mom is best described as a moon in action and a sun in radiance. A moon whose light is calm, peaceful and relaxing, beautiful and more often hidden and harmless, while her children, mini suns, a reflection of the light she carries. A queen of her kingdom, the loss of her King didn’t take away her dignity as a queen, she bravely carried the responsibility of life as a real queen, keeping, guiding, and managing her kingdom.

God gave me the grace and privilege to pause life and be with her during her last six months on Earth, I got the best gift to hold her hands few hours before her death and pray with her to make it right with her God in her heart and she gave a deep breath, held my hand tightly when I said Amen.

My heart bleeds beyond human imagination yet at peace for there is Hope beyond the grave.

Rest on my heroine. Until the resurrection morning, when I will hear her voice call me “TOCHI” again.

Engr. Imoh James

A tribute to my lovely mum

My first love, my mum, my tutor, My Chekuru, My paddy woman. I recall whenever I visit the neighbors or my sister will say “look your husband is here” and I’ll smile and hail you as usual “my chekuru international”.

The one that I call by whistling from a mile and she’ll smile even without seeing me. My introverted mum is in the crowd but extroverted mum with her children and people she feels safe with. My mum doesn’t raise hands on other people’s kids. The one that makes morning and evening devotional a lifestyle by singing to wake us up even when we murmur or grumble due to the sound sleep. Her voice and songs clear the air before the Bible reading and prayers sometimes.

The one that calls to remind me of calling my siblings, especially my younger one. The one that reports every matter to us as family. The one that leaves at peace with everyone in our neighborhood as the Bible recommends us to do.

May 25th, 2026, the night was quiet, but my heart was loud. The room held its breath while I held your arm and held you tight. I wish I could have seen death coming and fought it for you. I wish my hands were strong enough to keep you here, but death is inevitable till Jesus Christ comes again.

You cared for me with your meals, healed me like a hospital, and taught me satisfaction, hygiene and modesty in dressing. Your songs still lived in me. You left while I was holding on to you, and I’ve been holding on to your memory ever since. The scar is deep, but your love is deeper.

Your songs had gone silent, but I could still hear them in the space between your breaths. The lamp burned low, like it didn’t want to see you go.

Outside, the world kept moving, but in here time stopped. I watched death take you while my hands tried to give you back to life at 11:20 p.m. That was the night the house learned how to be empty.

The night your meals stopped coming, your hospitality went still, and your hope became my job to carry. I wish I could fight death. Instead, I’m left fighting the night without you.

Mum, you care in a way that makes the whole house feel safe. Even on my worst days, your concern wraps around me like a blanket. Then you sing like an Angel, and your voice turns ordinary moments into something warm like an Angel. Whether it’s a hymn while you cook or doing something else or sitting shaking your legs, your songs are the soundtrack of my childhood, teen and early adult.

Thank you for caring for all of us, and for filling our home with motherly love, care and Christian music. I love you to the moon and back as I usually tell you whenever I’m leaving, but God loves you most. Keep sleeping till we meet in the resurrection morning.

“To live in hearts we leave behind is not to die.”Thomas Campbell

Engr. Godspower James

A tribute to my mother — from her Eberechi, the one I photocopied from

There is a phone that will not ring tonight, and a silence where your voice used to live. Chidiebere, Eberechi, you dey there? You don eat? Hope say you dey rest? You done pray today? The line is open. No one calls me home. I was your handbag, your last and littlest thing, the child you carried close and would not set down. Now the handbag is set down, and the arm that held me has gone to sleep.

How many nights did you stay awake for me, praying and counting the clock, refusing your own rest, not closing your eyes until mine were safe till the journey ended, till I said, Mumsy, I don reach O. Mumsy, you have travelled now, gone to sleep and I am the one left awake, waiting to hear that you arrived safely home. Now we wait for that glorious morning.

I would lie across your lap, and we would talk, and talk, and talk, and then you would sing. You sang as though the morning could not start without you, song after song after song, and I, fool that I was, recorded none of them. I would have played them a whole month without stopping, a concert with no interval and no end, now I hold the empty hush where all that music was.

You knew my strange tastebuds, the small dust/smoke allergies, the things that did me harm. You guarded me from what I could not eat, from what I should not carry, who will read me so closely now? Who will know me before I even speak? And here is the grief I did not expect — losing you, I lost my father twice.

When he slept, the pain came only sparingly, a quiet ache that visited and left, but you, you opened the whole house of sorrow. For now it lands on me that I am alone, and now it lands that my father gave, and gave, and gave, poured himself out for everyone he loved, and never once sat down to be poured into. He never got to rest inside my care, never got to be the one looked after. I miss him more, standing here without you both, a child of two graves and an open sky.

Before the tears decide everything — I am sad, yes, sad that you are no more, mum. I am not without my pride, my quiet joy. You lived. You truly lived. You saw the children you suffered for rise and become something in this world. When sickness came, you were not abandoned to it, you were held, you were tended, you were loved out loud. You saw us love you. You knew. We did all we could, in every way we could, and you went to sleep knowing it.

So, beneath all this weeping there is a smile, small and sure, the satisfaction that your race was finished well, that you were carried at the end, the way you carried us at the beginning. This is where my sorrow turns to Joy.

I am holding to the promise as it is written, “the dead in Christ shall rise first.” So, you will rise before us all, strong and hearty, all your sickness gone for good, your face always was, still smiling, the very face that was photocopied to make mine. I will come to you with all my questions: “Shey, you had to sleep? Shey, you had to rest? Shey, the pain had grown so heavy that you had to let go?” Before you answer I will tell you this — I understand. Even as I missed you, I understood.

That night, when you left us, I cried, the longest hour of my whole life, alone and with no comfort, I understood. Even though you left sooner than I would have chosen, I understood. Then I will hug you and hold you and not let go and tell you how I missed you while you slept, and tell you the small confessions I have kept — that I wish I had called more, visited more, stayed longer in your room all those times I came home.

I know there are no tears up there, no sorrow allowed up yonder, so I wonder how I’ll say all this and not weep. Perhaps the incorruptible body simply will not let the teardrop fall. Well, we would see and know on that resurrection morning.

And oh, the double joy of that day — I will look to my side, and it will be you. I will look up, and it will be Jesus. Sideways, my mother. Upward, my Lord. I can feel the gladness of it even now, alive in my chest as I write this down, the morning when the grave gives back its sleepers and we rise to meet Him in the air.

That morning is not yet, and I am still here, with a life to live. So, I will not carry you as a wound. I will carry you as an anchor. Your prayers are not finished working, your every word of counsel still holds me steady as Christ leads me on. I will wait. I will hope. I will stand tall. I will live, not in the heavy grief that pulls me under, but in the brave remembering that lifts me up, so that when I speak your name, it comes out as a song, and not a sob.

Sleep, Mumsy. Sleep till that great day. Rest now, you have more than earned your rest. See you on that glad, glad morning, when, looking sideways, it will be you.

I LOVE YOU ALWAYS.

— Your Eberechi, your handbag still
“Those we love never truly leave us. There are things that death cannot touch.”Jack Thorne

Miss Victory Israel Chioma

Tribute to My Beloved Grandmother, “Mummy”

Today, I celebrate the life of my beloved grandmother, whom I fondly call Mummy. Though my heart aches because of your absence, I am grateful to God for the gift of having you in my life.

Mummy, you were love in its purest form. You cared for my brothers and me with a passion that words can hardly describe. Whenever we visited, you made sure we never lacked anything. From clothes to little treats and gifts, you always found a way to provide for us and make us feel special. Your generosity knew no bounds, and your home was always a place of comfort, warmth, and joy.

One of my fondest memories is how patiently you cared for me when I was younger. When you discovered that I wet the bed at night, you did not shame or scold me. Instead, you would gently wake me up so I could use the restroom. Even when accidents happened, your response was always filled with understanding and love. Looking back now, I realize how much patience and kindness it required, and I will forever cherish that memory.

You also gave me gifts that will stay with me for a lifetime. You taught me how to sing hymns. I can still picture those peaceful nights when you would sit and sing, your voice filling the room with worship, creating memories that remain precious treasures in our hearts.

Mummy, you were also my teacher in the kitchen. Many of the meals I know how to prepare today were learned from watching and helping you cook. Through those simple moments, you taught me not only recipes but also the value of patience, diligence, and caring for others.

One thing I will never forget is your smile. No matter the situation, your face carried a warmth that made everyone feel welcome. Your presence brought peace, and your love touched everyone around you. You were the true embodiment of your name, ‘LOVE.’

Although I will miss you deeply, I take comfort in knowing that your love lives on through your children, my uncles and aunts, and through all the lives you touched. The lessons you taught me, the hymns you sang, the meals you shared, and the love you freely gave will remain with me forever.

I am comforted by the assurance that you are resting with the Lord. We look forward to that glorious and joyous day when we shall all gather again in paradise, reunited in God’s presence.

Thank you, Mummy, for your love, your sacrifices, your wisdom, and your unwavering care. Your legacy will continue to live in our hearts. Rest peacefully, Mummy, until we meet again.

— With love and gratitude, Victory Israel Chioma

Master Joshua Israel

Tribute To My Beloved Grandmother

Today, I pay tribute to my beloved grandmother, a woman whose kindness, love, and caring nature made her truly extraordinary. Among all the people I have known, she lived an exemplary life. Her name was Love, and she embodied that virtue in every step she took.

From as far back as I can remember, she was always caring, welcoming, and full of love. One of the things I will miss most about her is her cooking. Her meals brought joy and made every gathering feel special. No matter the occasion, she was always prepared and ready to make everyone feel at home.

She had a kind heart and a warm spirit. She showed love not only through her words but through her actions. Her presence brought comfort, joy, and happiness to those around her, and she had a way of making people feel loved and appreciated.

Although her passing brings sadness, I am grateful for the time I shared with her and the lessons she taught me through the way she lived her life. Her kindness, generosity, and love will continue to inspire me and remain in my heart forever.

Grandma, thank you for your love, your care, and the beautiful memories you gave us. You will always be loved, remembered, and deeply missed. Rest peacefully in the bosom of the Lord.

— Your grandson, Joshua Israel Chioma

Master Emmanuel Israel

Tribute to Grandma

Grandma, your love was the quiet strength in our family. You taught us kindness without words, warmth without conditions, and courage without fanfare. Every meal, every story, every gentle correction shaped who we are. The world feels emptier without your laugh, but richer because you lived. We miss you; we thank you, and we’ll carry you with us always.

— Your grandson, Emmanuel

Miss Eudora Edidiong Godwin James

Tribute to my Lovely Grandma

I love grandma because she took care of my dad when he was a baby. Grandma use to protect me. Grandma use to call me Edi, I miss grandma because she died. I love granma’s garri and soup and her beans and plantain. Grandma died because she was old and sick. I wish grandma could come back. I hope Jesus will wake her up when he comes back. Jesus died and woke up in three days, so Jesus can wake her up when he comes back.

“Like a bird singing in the rain, let grateful memories survive in time of sorrow.”Robert Louis Stevenson

Mrs. Deborah Israel

A tribute to my mother-in-law

Mumsi, as I fondly called you, the news of your departure came to me as a shock that faithful morning. Hmm! Who are we to question God?

I didn’t know you’d be leaving so soon. One thing I will miss about you is your smile, even on your tough days you still wore a smile. You were welcoming, calm and loving.

Words fail me to describe who you were.

Rest well Mumsi, rest till we meet to part no more. Rest well, strong woman.

Mrs. Chiamaka Godwin James

Dear Mumsy

You were a caring woman whose kindness touched everyone around you.

Thank you for the love you gave, your time, the children you raised, and for allowing everyone to be themselves around you.

Your memories will forever be with us. Rest till Jesus comes.

Pastor Earnest Nwoko

Tribute to Our Beloved Mother

Ma Mère weren’t just a mother-in-law; she was a second mother, a confidante, and my greatest cheerleader — that’s why I call her ma mère. Each time I return from ministerial assignment, she hears my voice, even on her sick bed she starts smiling.

Ma Mère welcomed me with open arms and loved me as her own son. I am endlessly grateful for the remarkable woman you raised, and the even more amazing grandmother you were. Our family wouldn’t be the same without your shining light.

Thank you for your endless patience, your wise counsel, and the unconditional love you showed me from day one. You will forever hold a special place in my heart.

— Your son in law, Pastor Nwoko Ernest

Sir Chris Nnamdi Chioma, JP

Tribute To My Sister Adaibe

The most pressured member of the family is not the father or the mother in some families. It is the eldest daughter’s responsibilities — no one officially assigned to her, yet everyone expects her to fulfill.

The eldest daughter often grows faster than the rest of the children, while others are allowed to simply be kids. She is frequently placed in the role of a helper, caretaker. She helps raise her younger siblings, watches over them, settles their quarrels and sometimes even sacrifices her own time and dreams to make sure everyone is okay.

In many families, she becomes a second parent without noticing it. If the mother is tired, she steps in. If the father is strict, she becomes a mediator between him and the younger ones. When problems arise, she is expected to understand, adjust and remain strong. Her emotions are often pushed aside because she must be the mature one. There is also this invisible pressure of expectation — she must behave well so the family name is respected. She must not fail because everyone is watching her.

Despite all these pressures, her sacrifices are rarely acknowledged; people often assume she is strong enough to handle everything. They forget that she needs support too. The truth is that she often carries silent burdens for years without complaining. She is resilient and loyal to the family. This is your story, my sister.

I remember growing up how my mum would send you to the market in faraway towns and villages to sell farm produce and buy food items in return. You would listen and memorize a long list of items not written down anywhere. When you came back from the market, you would sit down with my mother and give account. Get them correctly, you are asked to start cooking. When you don’t, you are asked to go back and get the things you forgot. I used to think my mother was too harsh and unfair to you, not knowing that she was preparing you for the task ahead.

After your husband’s death, you raised all four children as graduates. You groomed and nurtured them to become responsible adults. Well done.

My friends and colleagues knew you as somebody they could report me to if I misbehaved. They knew Chioma’s sister lives at Onwuchekwa street. You had integrity and respect. I remember one day in the office, a contractor asked me and two of my colleagues to meet him during lunch break in his office at Geodetic road. When we got there, he gave us a brown envelope — you know what I mean. We couldn’t take the envelope back to the office because it couldn’t enter any of our pockets. We didn’t go with a car. Geodetic road was beside Shell gate and close to Onwuchekwa street where you lived. One of them suggested that we go to your house and give you the envelope to keep, because he knew that even if I came back to collect it, you would not release it to me until you saw all three of us. That was who you were — even strangers trusted you. After two days, I came to greet you, and you said, “I hope you didn’t come to take the envelope. Go and bring your friends.”

You were my confidant, my go-to person, my gist partner. I laughed a lot with you. I teased you a lot. You did not spare me either when I did something wrong. You told me to my face, “Nnamdi, that is not right, you should have done better.” You were kind to me. You loved me unconditionally. You were proud of me. It was you that took Diamond to school on his first day; the minders told me you refused to drop him and go home until after much persuasion.

I wish I told you how much I loved you. I know you would have been embarrassed if I did. You were spiritual and brave. Many people didn’t know this side of you, but I knew.

March 17, 2021, started as an ordinary day for me until you visited. When you came, you said, “Nnamdi, sit down, there is something I want to tell you.” I said okay, let’s go inside, and you said no, sit here at the veranda, let me talk to you, I don’t want anybody to distract us. “When the time comes bury me in your compound, I don’t want my church members to travel all the way to Akwa Ibom.” These were your words. I looked at you in shock. You were not sick, nothing serious other than the usual leg pain. I reminded you that your mates were still strong and agile and not thinking about death. You said you didn’t mean that anything would happen to you today or tomorrow. You told me, “Just in case.” Have you told your children? You said no, you would tell them later.

Then the next year the decline started, little by little. The banter reduced. Hospital visits increased. Your children hired caregivers. Whenever I came to visit, you would call me Nnamdi and ask about my children one after the other. And gradually, subsequent visits became monologue. You would look at me and say nothing. I was crying inside. I felt miserable.

One day I visited. I started giving you my gist, whether you heard me or not, whether you understood or not. I continued talking until I got tired and stood up to go, and one of the minders shouted, “Sir, she is smiling! Sir, she is smiling!” I looked, and I saw the beam, and I saw something else in your eyes. Your eyes spoke to me, and I remembered — you were saying, “Didn’t I tell you? Be strong, do what I asked you to do.”

The journey had begun. I would travel and would be praying, oh God, let nothing happen to my sister in my absence. I am glad God heard my prayer. I am glad you waited for me.

Now, that night. Oh, that long night! Ego and two of her children were there. PJ and her husband Earnest. Kufor, Goddy and Imoh. Stella, my wife. We all kept vigil. Then came the rain with vengeance, and people scampered for safety. I knew the angels were descending to take you home. I asked everyone around to go and sit in the car except your son, Imoh — the one I call mummy’s husband. As suddenly as it came, the rain stopped. A few minutes after, there was silence, and peace descended — the peace that passes all understanding. I looked at you, and I knew.

Sleep on, my sister; your memory will never fade with me. It will continue to live in time and space. You left everything and walked away. I guess you are in a better place now where there is no struggle, no sorrow, no pain.

Today, I carry out your wish. I fulfill that promise. I hope I did well. I thank your children for their cooperation. Goodbye my sister, and please, don’t go far away.

— Your Nnamdi
“They that love beyond the world cannot be separated by it. Death cannot kill what never dies.”William Penn

Evelyn Chioma

Tribute To My Dear Sister Dada!

Today I honour the memory of my dear elder sister, whose love, guidance and presence shaped my life in countless ways.

I remember that you played the role of both an elder sister and a nanny to us, your siblings. Our parents called you “Ada-Ibee,” meaning “The First Daughter of a Young Couple.” Everyone admired your fine, beautiful figure and called you “Ada-Ukwuu,” but I call you “Dada,” meaning “Big Sister.”

I am always accepted, safe and at peace whenever I come around you; the feeling is deeply personal. I had always wished to have a deep sister-to-sister conversation each time I visited you at the sick bed before your passing.

I miss you beyond words, my beloved sister. I will miss your phone calls, as you always called to say, “Ego, how are you and your children?”

With your passing, my beloved sister, our entire family is in great mourning, but I take my consolation in the word of God in 1 Corinthians 15:55, “O death, where is your sting? O grave, where is your victory?”

Sleep on, till the resurrection morning, when we shall meet to part no more.

— Your Sister, Ego

Innocent Chiama

Tribute To My Sister

Dada, it is difficult to accept that you are gone. This reminds me of Ecclesiastes 3:20, “We are made of dust and dust we shall all return.”

I will never forget the advice which you gave to me the last time you visited home — you said, “I should put hope in God,” that is something I will always recall.

May you remain in Jehovah’s memory till the resurrection day.

“Lives are like rivers: eventually they go where they must. Not where we want them to.”Richard Russo

Mrs. Stella Chioma

A Tribute To A Special Sister-in-Law

Adaibe, my friend, my gist partner, my sister-in-law that became my children’s grandmother — the person I watch movies with, gist and laugh with, waiting for the next episode to come. The person who took care of my children from the first to the fifth. The one who loved my family and became friends with my mum — if I were to weigh your importance to me it would be infinity over a hundred, the one I can confide in and be alright.

She made me feel like I’ve known her parents, because she told me stories from the past; she always called to know how I was doing. If I wanted to make her laugh, I called her Adaibe. We were so close that she visited home anytime she liked; she had a room in all our houses specially for her. She knew what my children loved, no matter how little it was, and I always appreciated that.

Her last visit was after my surgery. When she came to visit, she gave me a warm hug and started crying, saying “Thank God, thank God, thank God” — these were her special words to me anytime I gave birth. Things changed when she stopped calling and stopped receiving my calls… I asked myself if this was nature calling, so I visited, and I saw that God was preparing to take her. On that fateful night it showed. She was waiting for my husband and me to come, and then it rained heavily. After the rain, she gave up — that’s when it dawned on me that she had gone to be with the Lord.

— Adaibe, till we meet again. Yours, Stella
“In the end, it’s not the years in your life that count. It’s the life in your years.”Richard Russo

Mr. Diamond Chioma

A Tribute To My Beloved Aunt

Auntie, if there’s one word that keeps coming back to me when I think of you, it’s home. “Patience mother,” as we fondly called you.

You had this way of making any room feel warmer the second you walked in; your smile entered the room first, gentle and honest, and then you would follow it with arms that knew when a hug was needed. You didn’t just ask how I was — you waited for the real answer, and you remembered it. Years later you would still ask about that thing you told me years ago, is it going well… that’s who you were, you paid attention to every detail.

I keep replaying the little things you did. When I was small, my parents told me that on my first day of school you carried me on your accommodating shoulders; they said my legs didn’t touch the ground. I believe I remember this moment. You were my first means of transportation — that’s very special. You taught me without lecturing: don’t join bad friends, always be your own person, be humble. I remember how you spoiled me with the fruits I loved; you’d always come with them, not because it was for everyone, but because I said I loved them.

What amazes me is the love you and your brother, my father, had — it was so obvious to see. How you would say “I want to go see my brother,” how my dad, after church, would always want to see you before going home. Sometimes we wonder if we’ve been in church for hours, but he wants to see his sister… blood was always thicker than water.

Death doesn’t get the last word with someone like you; your stories have still been told every time your nieces and nephews gather. Your values are still shaping us. Every time I choose kindness over bitterness, patience over anger, I’m honouring you.

Thank you, Aunt, for loving me without conditions, thank you for being my soft place and mirror. We miss you terribly, we love you endlessly and we will keep making you proud. Rest well, Auntie, until we meet again.

— Your Diamond

Precious Ake

A Tribute to My Mrs. Love

My second mother. My babygirl keeper. They say not all angels have wings. Some of them come in the form of women who refuse to let you fall. That woman is you, Mrs. Love.

You didn’t birth me, but you birthed the woman I’m becoming. When my world went quiet, your voice became my lullaby. When I felt invisible, you looked me in the eye and said, “Babygirl, I see you. Precious girl, you matter.” Those two names — Babygirl, Precious Girl — are not just nicknames. They are prayers you spoke over my life on days I couldn’t pray for myself. They are reminders that even when I doubted my worth, you never did. In your presence, I learned what home feels like. Warmth without judgment. Correction without shame. Love without conditions.

You fed me when I was hungry, not just with food, but with wisdom. You always had food waiting for me when I came back tired from work — hot plate, soft words, and a seat at your table before I even asked. That was you telling me, “You’re safe now, Precious Girl,” without saying a word. You covered me with prayers I never heard but always felt. You celebrated my small wins like they were world records, and you held my hand through losses that would have broken me.

Mrs. Love, you taught me that a mother’s heart isn’t about DNA. It’s about who shows up. Who stays. Who calls you “Precious Girl” even when you feel like a mess. You chose me, again and again.

So today I don’t just say “thank you.” I say, thank you for being my safe place. Thank you for the late-night talks, the songs and prayers that saved me, healed me. Thank you for the meals that fed more than my stomach — they fed my soul. Thank you for proving that love can still be soft in a hard world. If I grow up to be half the woman you are — strong, kind, and full of grace — then my life would mean something.

I may not have called you “Mum” at birth, but my heart has called you that every day since. You are my Mrs. Love. My Babygirl’s guardian. My Precious Girl’s biggest cheerleader. I love you. I honour you. I will make you proud.

— Forever your Babygirl, Ake Precious

Bridget Amaza Bulus

To my grandma with the kindest heart

You may be gone from my sight, but never from my heart. Continue to rest in perfect peace, Mama. I love you forever. I wish I could see you again, to tell you goodbye. I miss you so much, Grandma.

“Say not in grief that she is no more, but live in thankfulness that she was.”
A Life In Pictures

Appreciation

The family of our beloved mother wishes to express our heartfelt gratitude to all relatives, friends, neighbours, church members, colleagues, and well-wishers for the love, prayers, visits, messages, financial support, and various acts of kindness shown to us during this period of bereavement.

Your compassion, encouragement, and support have been a great source of comfort and strength to us. We are deeply touched by your presence and the sacrifices you have made to mourn and celebrate her life with us. Though we may not be able to thank each person individually, please accept this message as an expression of our sincere appreciation. We pray that God richly blesses and rewards you for your kindness.

— Godwin James, for the Family

Deaconess Love James (Nwa-Ada) · 1954–2026

“Your life was a blessing, your memory a treasure, you are loved beyond words and missed beyond measure.”

Made with love, in her honour.
